title: Your First Program
description: Write and run your very first line of Python code!

slides.title: Time to Code! 🎉
slides.content:
# Let's Write Some Python!

You've learned what Python is — now it's time to actually write some code!

In this lesson, you'll:

* ✍️ Write your first Python program
* ▶️ Understand how to run code
* 🎯 Learn the famous "Hello, World!" tradition

slides.title: The Hello World Tradition 🌍
slides.content:
# A Programming Tradition Since 1972!

Every programmer's first program is **"Hello, World!"** — it's a tradition that started over 50 years ago!

**Why?** Because it's the simplest way to:

* ✅ Check that your setup works
* ✅ Learn the basic syntax
* ✅ Feel the joy of running code!

You're about to join millions of programmers who started exactly this way.

quizData.question: What is traditionally the first program every programmer writes?
quizData.explanation: Hello, World! is the traditional first program — a simple display of text that's been the starting point since 1972!

slides.title: Your First Program! 🚀
slides.content: Here it is — your very first Python program! This single line tells Python to display text on the screen:

slides.title: Breaking It Down 🔍
slides.content:
# Understanding the Code

Let's examine `print("Hello, World!")`:

* **print** — A built-in function that displays text
* **( )** — Parentheses contain what we want to print
* **"Hello, World!"** — The text inside quotes

Think of `print()` as Python's way of "speaking" to you!

quizData.question: What does the print() function do?
quizData.explanation: The print() function displays text on the screen — it doesn't actually print to paper!

slides.title: Quotes Matter! 📝
slides.content:
# Text Must Be in Quotes

In Python, text (called **strings**) must be wrapped in quotes:

* ✅ `"Hello"` — Double quotes work
* ✅ `'Hello'` — Single quotes work too
* ❌ `Hello` — No quotes = error!

Both single and double quotes work the same way. Just be consistent!

quizData.question: Which is the correct way to write text in Python?
quizData.explanation: Text must be wrapped in quotes (single or double), and passed to print() inside parentheses!

slides.title: Try Different Messages! 💬
slides.content: You can print any message you want! Try running this:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("I am learning Python!")
print("This is exciting!")

slides.title: Multiple Print Statements 📋
slides.content:
# Printing Multiple Lines

You can use multiple `print()` statements to display several lines:

```python
print("Line 1")
print("Line 2")
print("Line 3")
```

Each `print()` creates a new line in the output. Python runs them from top to bottom!

quizData.question: How does Python run multiple print() statements?
quizData.explanation: Python executes code from top to bottom, one line at a time!

slides.title: Common Mistakes ⚠️
slides.content:
## Watch Out For These Errors!

**1. Forgetting parentheses:**
* ❌ `print "Hello"` — Missing ()
* ✅ `print("Hello")`

**2. Mismatched quotes:**
* ❌ `print("Hello')` — Different quotes
* ✅ `print("Hello")`

**3. Wrong capitalization:**
* ❌ `Print("Hello")` — Capital P
* ✅ `print("Hello")` — lowercase p

quizData.question: Which of these will cause an error?
quizData.explanation: Print with a capital P is wrong! Python is case-sensitive — it must be lowercase print().

slides.title: Personalize It! 🎨
slides.content: Now try making the program personal! Run this code and imagine replacing 'Coder' with your name:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Hello, Coder!")
print("Welcome to Python!")
print("Let's build amazing things!")

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Experiment Fearlessly!

The best way to learn is by experimenting:

* Try changing the text inside the quotes
* Add more print() statements
* See what happens when you make a mistake

**Errors are your friends!** They teach you what works and what doesn't. Don't be afraid to break things!

quizData.question: What character must surround text in a print statement?
quizData.explanation: Text (strings) must be surrounded by quotes — either single ' ' or double " ".
